A mediastinal mass could be a tumor, possibly cancerous given his smoking history.
Adenopathy suggests lymph node enlargement, which can be due to infection, inflammation, or cancer spread.
Putting this together, the dyspnea and venous edema point towards a blockage in the venous system.
The mediastinal mass might be compressing the superior vena cava (SVC), leading to SVC syndrome.
SVC syndrome occurs when there's a blockage of the SVC, causing venous congestion in the upper half of the body.
This can cause swelling (edema) in the face, arms, and trunk, along with distended veins.
The facial erythema could be due to the venous congestion as well.
The mass in the mediastinum is likely the cause of the obstruction.
Given his smoking history, the mass is probably a malignancy, maybe a lung cancer that has spread to the mediastinum or lymph nodes.

So, considering all these factors—dyspnea, edema, venous congestion, mediastinal mass, and smoking history—the most likely diagnosis is Superior Vena Cava Syndrome (SVC syndrome) caused by a mediastinal mass, likely from lung cancer.
